title: Blackberry jam
subtitle: Late-summer hedgerow preserve.
excerpt: Blackberries cooked with sugar and lemon juice until set. The fruit gives a deep purple, almost-black jam with seeds suspended; sieve some of the cooked fruit through a fine sieve for a smoother jar. Makes about three 340 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Blackberry Jam'. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), describes the pectin-poor character of blackberry and the value of adding apple or lemon juice. USDA / NCHFP setting-point guidance: 104.5°C.

content.text: A kilo of foraged blackberries cooked gently with lemon juice until they collapse, then taken to setting point with sugar. The fruit's natural pectin sits at the low end of the scale; the lemon juice gives the acidity that lets the pectin set. Twenty to thirty minutes of boiling, three jars of deep purple jam.

Blackberries vary year to year. Wet summers produce fat watery fruit that takes longer to set; dry years give smaller, tarter berries that set faster. Pick a mix of black and slightly under-ripe red berries; the under-ripe ones carry more pectin.

items.name: Blackberries
items.prepNote: fresh, picked over for stems and leaves

content.text: Tip {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar into a roasting tray and warm in a 140°C oven for 10 minutes.

Sit {{blackberries}} of blackberries in the jam pan with {{lemon}} of lemon juice and 100 ml of water. Cook gently over low heat for 15 minutes, stirring now and then, until the fruit has collapsed and the juice runs deep purple-black. The skins should be tender enough to crush against the side of the pan with a wooden spoon.
Optional: sieve some of the seeds

If you want a smoother jam, pass half the cooked fruit through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing with the back of a spoon. Tip the sieved purée back into the pan with the other half-uncooked fruit. Skip this step for a chunky seedy jam.
Tip the warm sugar into the pan over low heat. Stir until every grain has dissolved. Don't rush; undissolved sugar crystallises the finished jam.
Bring to a rolling boil. Boil 10 to 15 minutes until the temperature hits 104.5°C or the cold-saucer test wrinkles when pushed with a fingertip. Skim any dark foam from the surface.
Off the heat. Rest 10 minutes; the fruit settles back through the syrup. Ladle into hot sterilised jars. Seal with lids; tighten when warm. Cool, label, store in a cool dark cupboard.

items.fix: Boil another 5 minutes. Test again. Adding a chopped Bramley apple at the fruit stage gives extra pectin.
items.cause: Didn't reach setting point; blackberries low in pectin.

items.fix: Warm the open jar in a water bath. Pull from the heat at the first wrinkle next time.
Dissolve patiently over low heat before any rolling boil.
items.cause: Sugar not fully dissolved before the boil.
items.symptom: Crystallised after a few weeks in the jar

items.fix: Rest 10 minutes off the heat before ladling.
items.symptom: Fruit on top, syrup on bottom

content.text: Blackberry jam is the late-summer hedgerow tradition. The fruit ripens on roadside brambles through August and September; an hour with a basket fills a pan. Spread on hot scones with clotted cream, layered into a Bakewell tart, or spooned through Greek yoghurt.
